text:
While tailored synthetic methods are generally required to build specific natural products, in many cases the diversity of natural products is produced through the divergent cyclization of a common precursor, in this case a common polyunsaturated precursor. We herein demonstrate a general and versatile approach for the synthesis of monoterpene indole alkaloids (MIAs) through divergent cyclization of a common dihydropyridine-vinylindole precursor. This precursor was produced in a few steps from tryptamine using standard manipulations and conditions to switch on and off its reactivity. Through the use of this unique precursor we were able to prepare in 6-9 steps and in good yields five different types of MIA skeletons including aspidosperma, iboga, andranginine and ngouniensine structures as well as a new artificial type.

We report a modular and flexible “assembly line” approach based on the key copper(I)-catalyzed 6-endo-dig cyclization of a diene to form the dihydropyridine core of the indole alkaloids. Within this assembly line, we have demonstrated three different modes of [4+2] cyclizations and two redox-mediated annulations. To showcase the efficiency and versatility of the strategy, we have used it for the total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine and (±)-andranginine as well as (−)-catharanthine, the last one being the shortest enantiocontrolled synthesis reported to date. Our results provide a programmable, efficient, and flexible diversification method for complex structures and give us a tool to emulate biogenetic divergence in the laboratory.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
